topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. Tucked inside a dusty old shoebox in the attic, beneath moth-eaten linens, was a meticulously organized collection of every single assignment I had ever turned in. Calculus proofs, Shakespearean essays, even that dreadful history project from seventh grade—all perfectly preserved, labeled with my name in my mother's elegant, looping script. I stared at the stack, my hands trembling slightly. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I walked downstairs, the silence of the house suddenly deafening. When she finally appeared, humming softly in the kitchen, I didn't speak immediately. I simply held up the box, the paper crinkling softly under my grip. My jaw tightened, and the set of my shoulders seemed to retract inward, a clear signal that something fundamental had been disrupted. "Where did you keep all this?" My voice was flat, devoid of its usual warmth, a noticeable edge beneath the surface.

[story 2]

I found the evidence hidden in a seldom-used filing cabinet in the spare room. It wasn't just a few papers; it was a chronological archive of my academic life, every completed task cataloged with unnerving precision. As I ran my fingers over the crisp edges of a particularly detailed chemistry report, a knot tightened painfully in my chest. I felt a sudden, sharp need to retreat. I turned to face my mother, who was reading a book nearby, a picture of serene composure. I couldn't bring myself to look directly at her face. Instead, I focused intently on a speck of dust on the wooden floor, my gaze unwavering and cold. "This is… unnecessary," I managed, the words clipped and sharp. The air around me felt thick, heavy, as if I were struggling to draw a full breath. I took a step back, my posture rigid, a visible barrier erected between us.

[story 3]

It was a treasure trove of my past, tucked away in an old cedar chest. Every homework sheet, every graded paper, every forgotten test—it was all there, preserved like artifacts. A wave of intense irritation washed over me, so potent it made my skin prickle. I picked up a crumpled essay from middle school, the ink slightly faded. I felt a sudden, hot pressure building behind my eyes. When my mother walked into the room, smiling benignly, I didn't look up. I simply dropped the papers back into the chest with unnecessary force. I stood by the doorway, arms crossed tightly over my chest, my breathing shallow and quick. "I didn't ask for this," I stated, my tone laced with a distinct lack of regard. The tension radiating from me was palpable; I waited, refusing to meet her eyes, for her reaction to the sheer audacity of the collection.
